one_line_takeaway: CAMP is a middleware framework that tracks a session-level co-occurrence graph of PII entity types across multi-turn LLM conversations and retroactively pseudonymizes the full history once a cumulative risk score crosses a threshold, cutting exposed PII entity types to zero versus 3-6 for a per-turn Presidio baseline across four synthetic scenarios -- but this is demonstrated only on hand-built synthetic conversations with no formal privacy guarantee or quantitative utility metric.
key_finding: At tau=2.0 (Table IV), CAMP reduced real PII entity-type exposure to the external model to zero in all four scenarios, while the per-turn Presidio baseline exposed 5 (S1), 6 (S2), 3 (S3), and 5 (S4) distinct entity types. Final CPE scores at session end were 6.82 (S1), 9.25 (S2, the highest, from name+location+salary+age+ethnicity accumulating within 7 turns), 8.03 (S3), and 7.48 (S4). CAMP's intervention (retroactive pseudonymization) triggered at turn 4 (S1), turn 2 (S2), turn 2 (S3), and turn 5 (S4). The threshold-sensitivity sweep (Table V) showed S1's trigger turn was invariant to tau (turn 4 at all three settings, suggesting a natural accumulation 'cliff'); S3 was the most threshold-sensitive (turn 2 to turn 3 between tau=2.0 and 2.5); S4 showed the widest range (turn 3 at tau=1.5 vs. turn 5 at tau=2.0/2.5). The paper reports (qualitatively, not with a numeric utility metric) that in all four scenarios the LLM produced coherent, contextually appropriate post-pseudonymization responses and the de-masking layer restored original identities in all responses with no synthetic substitutes leaking into final output.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is CAMP (Cumulative Agentic Masking and Pruning), a middleware framework for multi-turn LLM conversations that (1) formally defines 'Cumulative PII Exposure' (CPE), a session-level risk score computed as the sum of amplified entity-type weights w(v)*(1+alpha*deg(v)) over a co-occurrence graph built from PII entity types seen so far in a session, (2) maintains a stateful session-level PII registry (contrasted explicitly with per-turn maskers like Presidio, which discard entity information after each turn), (3) performs retroactive pseudonymization of the ENTIRE conversation history (using the Faker library for consistent synthetic substitutes) once CPE(t) crosses a configurable threshold tau, (4) applies hard-block rules that always strip SSN/credit card/bank account/IBAN spans as [BLOCKED] regardless of the CPE score, and (5) a local de-masking layer that reverses the pseudonym map on the LLM's response before returning it to the user, so the user sees real identities while the external LLM only ever sees synthetic ones. The five components together (extractor, registry, co-occurrence graph/scorer, retroactive pseudonymizer, de-masker) constitute the system; the extractor itself is built on off-the-shelf Microsoft Presidio with added custom recognizers, not a paper contribution.
threat_model: Adversary is any entity with access to the third-party LLM API's request logs, who attempts re-identification by combining PII fragments scattered across the forwarded conversation history. The user is assumed to disclose PII naturally in the course of legitimate task completion (not an attacker). CAMP is deployed as a trusted local middleware layer by the operator; the external hosted LLM is treated as untrusted. The re-identification threshold tau is a configurable parameter, evaluated at tau in {1.5, 2.0, 2.5}. This is a data-exfiltration-to-a-trusted-but-observed-third-party threat model (mosaic-effect/re-identification risk), not an adversarial-prompting or model-extraction threat model.
limitations: All limitations below are stated by the paper's own Discussion/Future Directions sections, not inferred. (1) Evaluation is on only four hand-constructed synthetic scenarios, not real conversational data -- the paper explicitly lists 'Evaluation on real conversational data' as future work. (2) CAMP detects only PII that its Presidio-based extractor successfully identifies; contextually embedded or implicit disclosures (e.g., describing a workplace without naming the employer) are not detected and do not contribute to the CPE score -- a limitation the authors call 'fundamental' and shared with all NER-based approaches. (3) No systematic sensitivity study of the amplifier parameter alpha (fixed at 0.3 throughout) was performed; the paper flags this as future work. (4) Pseudonym quality is generic (Faker-based), which may be insufficient where LLM response quality depends on realistic-looking substitutes. (5) No formal privacy guarantee (e.g., differential privacy) is provided -- only heuristic protection via consistent substitution; integrating formal DP is listed as future work. (6) The paper reports no quantitative utility/quality metric for post-pseudonymization LLM responses (e.g., no BLEU/task-success/human rating), only a qualitative claim of coherence. (7) Single-agent, single-session scope only -- extending tracking across multi-agent delegation boundaries is explicitly deferred to future work (citing AgentLeak).

datasets_used: Four hand-constructed synthetic multi-turn conversation scenarios authored by the paper: S1 Healthcare (8 turns), S2 HR/Hiring (7 turns), S3 Finance (8 turns), S4 General (10 turns) -- not a public or pre-existing dataset
quantitative_results: CAMP exposed 0 real PII entity types to the external LLM in all four scenarios at tau=2.0 (Table IV)
Presidio per-turn baseline exposed 5 (S1), 6 (S2), 3 (S3), 5 (S4) distinct PII entity types (Table IV)
Final CPE scores at tau=2.0: S1=6.82, S2=9.25 (highest), S3=8.03, S4=7.48 (Table IV)
CAMP intervention/trigger turn at tau=2.0: S1 turn 4, S2 turn 2, S3 turn 2, S4 turn 5 (Table IV)

Threshold sensitivity (Table V): S1 triggers at turn 4 for all tau in {1.5,2.0,2.5}; S2 triggers at turn 2 for all tau; S3 triggers at turn 2 for tau=1.5/2.0 and turn 3 for tau=2.5; S4 triggers at turn 3 for tau=1.5 and turn 5 for tau=2.0/2.5

Combination amplifier alpha fixed at 0.3 across all experiments; entity base weights per Table II (e.g., SSN=1.00, Credit Card=1.00, DOB=0.90, Medical=0.85, Email=0.80, Phone=0.75, Person=0.60, Salary=0.60, Location=0.50, IP=0.50, Organization=0.30)
baselines_compared: Per-turn Microsoft Presidio masking (the paper's own re-implementation/baseline used for comparison, with custom domain recognizers added to the extractor also used inside CAMP itself)
